children.value: After downloading an NWB file, you
want to view the data inside. You can do basic reading of the file with
. This is a package designed to utilize, modify, and process NWB files. The basic read functionality of PyNWB is shown below.
Note: If running on a new environment, run this cell once and then restart the kernel

try:
    import databook_utils
except:
    !git clone --depth 1 https://github.com/AllenInstitute/openscope_databook.git
    %cd openscope_databook
    %pip install -e .
    %cd ./docs/basics

import os
import pandas as pd
from dandi import dandiapi, download
from pynwb import NWBHDF5IO
from hdmf_zarr.nwb import NWBZarrIO
To read an NWB File, it must first be downloaded.

may be changed to select a different file off of DANDI. If the file of interest is already downloaded, you don’t need to run the download cell again. When trying to download an embargoed file, refer to the code from the

filename = dandi_filepath.split("/")[-1]
filepath = f"{download_loc}/{filename}"

print(filename)
print(filepath)

client = dandiapi.DandiAPIClient()
my_dandiset = client.get_dandiset(dandiset_id)
file = my_dandiset.get_asset_by_path(dandi_filepath)

if not os.path.exists(filepath):
    # Download the file from DANDI to a local path
    file.download(filepath)
    print(f"Downloaded file to {filepath}")
else:
    print("File already exists")

# change pandas settings to show whole table
pd.set_option('display.max_rows', None)
pd.set_option('display.width', None)
pd.set_option('display.max_colwidth', None)
You can read in a NWB file with
method to retrieve an io object. You can use the
method to actually read it in. From there, you can see the raw data of the NWB file and can print the fields you are interested in.

# takes relative or absolute filepath as first argument
io = NWBHDF5IO(f"{download_loc}/{filename}", mode="r", load_namespaces=True)
nwb = io.read()
nwb

children.value: This example uses DANDI API calls directly to download a Zarr-backed NWB store and then opens it with

zarr_dandiset_id = "000719"
zarr_dandi_filepath = "icephys_DS_11_21_24/sub-1214579789_ses-1214621812_icephys_DirectoryStore.nwb.zarr"

# With preserve_tree=True, DANDI writes to: <output_dir>/<dandiset_id>/<asset_path>
zarr_filepath = os.path.join(download_loc, zarr_dandiset_id, zarr_dandi_filepath)

zarr_dandiset = client.get_dandiset(zarr_dandiset_id)
zarr_file = zarr_dandiset.get_asset_by_path(zarr_dandi_filepath)

if not os.path.exists(zarr_filepath):
    # Zarr assets are directory stores, so use dandi.download with preserve_tree
    download.download(
        zarr_file.download_url,
        output_dir=download_loc,
        preserve_tree=True,
    )
    print(f"Downloaded Zarr store to {zarr_filepath}")
else:
    print("Zarr store already exists")

if not os.path.exists(zarr_filepath):
    raise FileNotFoundError(f"Zarr store not found at expected path: {zarr_filepath}")

zarr_io = NWBZarrIO(path=zarr_filepath, mode="r")
zarr_nwb = zarr_io.read()
zarr_nwb;

### uncomment these to view aspects of the file

### not all of these exist for all NWB files (Key Errors will arise if the fields don't exist for this file)

# nwb.identifier
# nwb.processing
# nwb.acquisition["events"]
# nwb.intervals["trials"]
# nwb.stimulus["StimulusPresentation"]
# nwb.electrodes
OpenScope works with two main types of brain data files for different experiments. There’s Extracellular Electrophysiology,
probes to gather electrical data, and Optical Physiology,

Calcium imaging to gather fluorescence data. Since the NWB Standard is very flexible, and these files are used for different needs, there are several differences between these data types. These table schemas were made as comprehensive descriptions of what goes in our NWB files. Below,

is used to display a general outline for each file type where every field within the NWB file is described. Note that NWB files or files from other institutions could vary from these significantly. These tables are just meant to be used as a reference, don’t be overwhelmed trying to understand all the fields!

# change pandas settings to show whole table
pd.set_option('display.max_rows', None)
pd.set_option('display.width', None)
pd.set_option('display.max_colwidth', None)
Note that this is the schema for the older OpenScope NWBs, and does not necessarily apply to newer ones
